content.text: Writing about what you build is one of the highest-leverage activities in a technical career. A well-written post about an agent project reaches audiences that the code alone never will — hiring managers, potential collaborators, newsletter editors, conference organizers. And the discipline of explaining a system in writing inevitably reveals gaps in your own understanding that the code obscured.
What It Is

Writing and sharing in the context of an agentic AI portfolio means producing clear, specific, honest technical content about the systems you build and the insights you gain — and distributing that content through channels where the right people will find it.

The writing can take many forms: a detailed blog post about how you built your research agent and what failed, a short LinkedIn thread about a specific prompt engineering lesson, a GitHub README that is genuinely worth reading, a Substack post about a pattern you discovered, or a conference talk proposal built on your portfolio work.

The common thread is specificity. The writing that builds a reputation is the writing that teaches something concrete — a specific failure mode, a counterintuitive finding, a hard-won architectural insight. General overviews of topics that are already well-covered add nothing.
Why It Matters

Technical writing compounds in ways that code alone does not. A well-titled blog post continues to surface in search results for years. A newsletter post shared by one reader reaches that reader's entire network. A thread on X that resonates can reach tens of thousands of practitioners in a domain you care about.

Writing also creates social proof that is qualitatively different from GitHub stars. A post that 200 engineers found useful enough to bookmark signals that your thinking is worth following — which is the exact signal that opens doors to job opportunities, conference invitations, and collaborations.
Benefits
Clarifies your thinking:
the act of explaining a system in writing forces precision. Vague ideas that worked 'somehow' must be stated clearly. This process reveals gaps that code can hide.
Builds discoverability:
a blog post with a specific, searchable title is findable for years. Code repositories require someone to already know to look for them.
Establishes expertise:
consistent writing on a specific topic (e.g., multi-agent coordination, eval design, RAG pipelines) builds a recognizable association between your name and that domain.
Creates networking leverage:
people who engage with your writing become warm leads for professional relationships. It inverts the cold-contact dynamic.
Generates feedback:
a published post receives comments, corrections, and expansions from readers who know things you do not. The best posts start a conversation.
Compounds over time:
a body of writing accumulates into a body of work that is browsable, searchable, and citable — a track record that grows while you work on other things.
Trade-offs
Time cost:
a thorough technical post takes 4–12 hours to write, edit, and format well. This is time not spent building.
Accuracy risk:
an error in a widely-read technical post is embarrassing and can undermine credibility. Inaccurate code examples are especially dangerous.
Quality bar variation:
the posts that get shared are much better than the posts that get ignored. The distribution is extremely skewed — one excellent post outperforms ten adequate ones.
Platform dependency:
Medium can deprecate features, Substack can change its algorithm, Twitter/X can change its rules. Your canonical home should be a domain you own.
How It Works
Effective technical writing follows a pattern:
Start with a specific lesson:
not 'I built an agent', but 'here is the specific thing I learned about eval design that took me three days to figure out'. The lesson should be teachable in 1500–3000 words.
Show your work:
include the code, the trace, the eval result, the error message. Concrete artifacts make abstract lessons credible.
State what failed:
the writing that practitioners share most is the writing that honestly documents failures and what they taught. Success-only posts feel like marketing.
Edit ruthlessly:
first drafts are always too long and too unfocused. Every paragraph should earn its place. Cut anything that does not teach the specific lesson.
Choose the right channel:
publish on your own blog for canonical ownership; cross-post to Medium or dev.to for discovery; share excerpts on LinkedIn or X for immediate distribution.

attrs.alt: Writing pipeline: start from agent project with code, traces, and evals → extract a specific insight → draft and edit → publish across multiple channels (blog, Medium/Substack, LinkedIn/X, GitHub README)

content.text: Architecture
A sustainable writing setup has these components:
Personal blog (owned domain):
the canonical source for all your posts. Use a static site generator (Astro, Hugo, Gatsby) or a platform like Ghost. Own the domain; never use a platform's subdomain as your primary URL.
Distribution channels:
cross-post to Medium, dev.to, or Hashnode for built-in discovery. Summarize and link on LinkedIn and X for professional network distribution.
Newsletter (optional but powerful):
a Substack or Buttondown newsletter with direct subscribers is the most reliable distribution channel you can build. Platform algorithm changes do not affect email delivery.
Content calendar (minimal):
even a simple list of 'next five post ideas' prevents the blank-screen problem and ensures you always have a next post queued.
When to Use It
Write a post when:
You just finished a project and can identify one concrete lesson that took genuine effort to learn.
You encountered a surprising failure mode or counterintuitive result that other builders would benefit from knowing about.
You compared two approaches and reached a clear conclusion about when to use each.
You implemented a pattern that is commonly discussed but rarely shown with real code.
How to Implement

# Blog post outline template for an agent project write-up

## Title
# How I [specific action] to [specific result]: [brief counterintuitive finding]
# Example: "How I reduced my research agent's hallucination rate by 60% 
#           using an evaluation-driven loop"

## Opening paragraph (2-3 sentences)
- State the specific problem you faced
- State the non-obvious solution or finding
- State why it matters to the reader

## Section 1: The Problem (what you were trying to solve)
- Describe the specific task and why you chose it
- Include the eval metric you used to measure success
- Show the initial baseline (pass rate, latency, cost)

## Section 2: What You Tried First (and why it didn't work)
- This is the most valuable section
- Show the failure, not just the solution
- Include specific error messages, traces, or eval numbers

## Section 3: The Key Insight
- State the insight clearly in 2-3 sentences
- Explain *why* it works (first principles, not magic)

## Section 4: The Implementation
- Show the relevant code (minimal, runnable)
- Include the before/after eval numbers

## Section 5: Limitations and Next Steps
- What your approach doesn't handle
- What you would do with more time

## Code snippet formatting rules
# - Every code snippet must run as-is or be clearly marked as pseudocode
# - Include language identifiers in fenced blocks
# - Use real model names, not "model_name"
# - Avoid API keys in examples (use os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])
Examples
Strong technical posts in the agentic AI space:
'Why my agent kept hallucinating tool results and how I fixed it':
forensic analysis of a specific failure mode, with traces showing the agent's reasoning before and after the fix.
'RAG vs. full context: when the extra cost is worth it':
a controlled comparison of two retrieval strategies on a specific benchmark, with real numbers.
'Building an eval harness from scratch':
a tutorial that shows the exact code for setting up a benchmark, logging results, and tracking improvement — a topic many builders need but few have written about clearly.
'Multi-agent systems: what I learned after 3 months of experiments':
a retrospective that identifies patterns, anti-patterns, and open questions from real project experience.
'The prompt that took me two weeks to get right':
a deep dive into the iteration process for a specific system prompt, showing rejected versions and explaining why each failed.
Real-World Example

A practitioner builds a coding agent that uses code execution to iteratively fix bugs. During development, they discover that the agent reliably fails when the test error message contains stack traces longer than 2000 characters — the relevant information is buried in truncated context. They fix this by preprocessing error messages to extract only the final failure line and the first relevant user frame.

The blog post: 'Why my coding agent was failing on 40% of inputs (and the 5-line fix)'. The post shows the trace log of a failing run, explains why long stack traces cause the failure, shows the preprocessing code, and includes before/after eval numbers (60% → 95% pass rate on the affected case class).

This post gets shared in two LangChain Discord servers and bookmarked by 150 engineers because it identifies a specific, surprising problem and shows a concrete fix. Three months later, a hiring manager at an AI startup messages the author after finding the post through a Google search.
Common Mistakes
Writing before the project is done:
a post about a project you haven't finished yet lacks the honest failures and concrete results that make posts worth reading.
Too general:
'Lessons learned from building an AI agent' competes with hundreds of similar posts. 'Why asyncio.gather broke my multi-agent orchestrator at scale' is findable and specific.
Code that doesn't run:
including broken, pseudocode-y, or incomplete code examples without marking them clearly destroys credibility. Every code block must either run as-is or be explicitly labeled as illustrative.
No failure cases:
a post that only shows the success path reads as marketing. The failures are the most valuable content.
Not editing:
a first draft is always 40% longer than it needs to be. Every paragraph that does not teach the specific lesson should be cut.
Platform-only distribution:
publishing only on Medium without your own blog means the post lives on someone else's platform. Always publish canonically on a domain you own.
Best Practices
One specific lesson per post:
posts that try to cover everything cover nothing. Identify the one thing the reader should understand when they finish, and build the entire post around that.
Start with the conclusion:
state the key finding in the first paragraph. Practitioners who are skimming decide whether to read based on the opening. Do not bury the finding.
Use real numbers:
'significant improvement' is meaningless. '67% reduction in hallucination rate' is specific and believable.
Show the eval:
if your post makes a claim about improvement, show the benchmark that measured it. This is the single biggest differentiator between credible and vague technical posts.
Link to your code:
every post about a project should link to the GitHub repository. The post drives traffic to the code; the code drives credibility to the post.
Cross-post strategically:
publish on your blog first (canonical), wait 2–3 days, then cross-post to Medium or dev.to with a canonical URL header to avoid SEO duplication.
Write a thread version:
a 5-tweet or 5-LinkedIn-post thread summarizing your post extends reach dramatically. Each item in the thread should be interesting standalone.
Performance & Scalability
Writing at scale (publishing consistently over months and years) requires a system:
Capture insights in real time:
keep a running note (Obsidian, Notion, a plain text file) of interesting findings as you build. The best posts come from notes taken during the project, not recalled afterward.
Batch editing:
write multiple drafts in the same session; edit them in a separate session days later. Distance improves editing quality.
Reuse and extend:
a strong post can become a conference talk, a newsletter issue, a chapter in a future guide, or a set of shorter threads. Plan for reuse when you write.
Track what resonates:
review your analytics quarterly. Which posts got the most traffic? The most bookmarks? Most shares? Write more in that direction.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Platform
Use Case
Notes
Ghost / Astro / Hugo
Self-hosted blog (canonical)
Own your domain and content
Substack
Newsletter + blog hybrid
Built-in subscriber management
Medium / dev.to
Discovery and cross-posting
Bring readers back to your canonical blog
Hashnode
Dev-focused blog with community
Good for agentic AI audience
LinkedIn Newsletter
Professional network reach
High visibility for job-seeking
Carbon / ray.so
Beautiful code screenshot embeds
For social media code sharing
Comparison with Alternatives
Talking at meetups (instead of writing):
talks reach a limited in-person audience; posts are global and permanent. Do both — but the talk should reference the post.
Recording videos (instead of writing):
video is more engaging but harder to search, harder to skim, and takes longer to produce. Write first, record a walkthrough after.
GitHub README only:
a README is an excellent form of writing but reaches only people who already found the repo. Blog posts create the path to the repo.
Social media threads only:
threads are ephemeral and hard to search. They are excellent for distribution but a poor canonical home. Always link to the full post.
Security & Reliability
Writing security considerations:
Never include real API keys:
review every code snippet before publishing. Even if a key appears in a comment as a placeholder, remove it.
Avoid revealing production details:
if you write about your work project, check what is confidential. Architectural details, customer data, and proprietary techniques may be off-limits.
Canonical URL headers when cross-posting:
always set the canonical URL to your blog when cross-posting to Medium or dev.to to prevent SEO splitting.
Archive your posts:
submit important posts to the Wayback Machine so they survive even if your hosting fails.
Mental Model

Think of writing as teaching the version of yourself from six months ago. That person just started the project you are now writing about. They are smart and motivated but do not yet know the specific thing you learned. Write exactly the post that would have saved them the most time.

This mental model solves the two most common writing problems: it eliminates over-explanation of basic concepts (your past self knew them) and it prevents omitting the specific, hard-won insight (your past self needed it most).

A complementary model: every project generates 3–5 specific lessons. Each lesson is one post. Write the post immediately after learning the lesson, while the experience is fresh. Six months later you will have an archive of posts that collectively document a deep technical journey — which is exactly what hiring managers and collaborators want to see.
Key Takeaways
Write about specific, hard-won lessons — not general overviews. Specificity is what makes a post findable, shareable, and worth reading.
Show failures, not just successes. Honest failure analysis is the content practitioners share most.
Include real numbers from your evals. 'Significant improvement' is meaningless; '67% reduction' is memorable.
Publish canonically on a domain you own; cross-post for discovery.
Link every post to its code repository and every repository to its post.
One specific lesson per post. Ruthlessly cut everything that doesn't teach that lesson.
Writing compounds: each post makes the next project more visible, and each project generates the next post.
Building a Consistent Writing Practice

The most important writing habit is regularity over quality. A post published every three weeks that is 'good enough' produces far more compounding value than a post every three months that is 'perfect'. Quality improves with volume — the 20th post you write will be significantly better than the first, regardless of how much time you spent on each.

A practical system: after finishing each project sprint (a week of significant building), spend 30 minutes capturing notes in a 'post ideas' document. Write down: what surprised you, what failed, what you would do differently, what you wish had existed. These notes are the raw material for future posts. When it is time to write, pick the note with the most specific lesson and expand it.

The hardest part of writing is starting. A simple technique: write the outline first (just bullet points for each section), then expand each bullet into a paragraph in a separate session. Outlines take 15 minutes; expansion takes 2–3 hours but is much easier than starting from a blank page.
Measuring Writing Effectiveness

Not all traffic metrics matter equally. A post that drives 200 visitors and produces 10 meaningful replies is more effective than a post that drives 2000 visitors and produces none. For career purposes, the most valuable metrics are:
Email subscribers gained:
email subscribers have explicitly chosen to hear from you. Each subscriber represents a warm relationship.
Quality of inbound contacts:
did the post generate messages from people you actually want to talk to? A hiring manager from a company you admire reaching out is worth more than 1000 page views.
External shares:
when a newsletter curator, conference organizer, or community moderator shares your post with their audience, that is the highest form of engagement.
Post-specific search traffic over time:
using Google Search Console, track which posts are gaining organic search traffic month over month. Those are the posts generating sustained value.

Review these metrics quarterly rather than daily. Daily traffic checks produce anxiety without actionable information. Quarterly reviews reveal which topics resonate, which formats perform best, and where to invest more writing time.
"Write the post that would have saved you the most time six months ago. That is the post that will save other practitioners the most time today."
